articles.title: Hyderabad Weather on May 13: IMD Warns of Rising Heatwave Conditions Across Telangana - NativePlanet
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Telangana
articles.full_text:
Residents in Hyderabad and several districts across Telangana are expected to experience hot and dry weather conditions on May 13, 2026, as the India Meteorological Department (IMD), Hyderabad, warned of intensifying heatwave conditions over the next few days.
According to the latest IMD forecast, maximum temperatures across the state are likely to rise by 2°C to 4°C during the coming four days. While a few districts may receive temporary relief through isolated thunderstorms and gusty winds, the broader weather pattern points towards increasing daytime heat across Telangana.
Hyderabad Weather Today
Hyderabad is expected to witness a maximum temperature of around 38°C to 39°C on Wednesday, while the minimum temperature is likely to remain near 26°C to 27°C.
Morning hours are expected to stay relatively warm before temperatures climb sharply by noon. Forecast data indicates temperatures may hover between 37°C and 38°C during the afternoon hours, making outdoor conditions uncomfortable, especially between 12 p.m. and 4 p.m.
Evening weather is expected to remain warm, with temperatures gradually dropping to around 30°C to 33°C after sunset. Weather conditions are likely to remain mostly clear with periodic cloud cover. No major rainfall activity is currently expected in the city.
Wind speeds are likely to remain mild at around 6 kmph, while humidity levels may stay elevated during early morning and late evening hours.
Districts Likely to See Thunderstorm Activity
The IMD stated that isolated thunderstorms and gusty winds reaching speeds of 30-40 kmph may affect a few Telangana districts on Wednesday.
Areas Likely to Receive Temporary Relief
Despite these isolated weather activities, dry and hot conditions are expected to dominate most parts of the state through the week.
Low-Pressure System Over Bay of Bengal
Meteorologists have also noted the presence of a low-pressure area over the southwest Bay of Bengal along with an associated cyclonic circulation extending up to 4.5 km above mean sea level.
According to the weather department, the system is expected to intensify further over the next 48 hours. A trough extending from the low-pressure region through north Tamil Nadu, Rayalaseema and north interior Karnataka is also influencing regional weather conditions.
However, the impact over Telangana is currently expected to be limited mainly to isolated cloud formation and brief thunderstorm activity in select districts.
Heatwave Advisory for Residents
With temperatures continuing to rise, residents have been advised to avoid prolonged exposure to direct sunlight during peak afternoon hours. People travelling outdoors are encouraged to stay hydrated, wear light clothing and avoid strenuous physical activity during the hottest part of the day.
Senior citizens, children and people with existing health conditions are expected to remain particularly vulnerable to heat-related illnesses as temperatures intensify across the state.

articles.title: Beat the heat: Dr Vamsee Krishna shares life saving tips about sunstroke - The Hans India
articles.search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Telangana
articles.full_text:
Dr Vamsee Krishna P is a highly renowned and experienced physician and diabetologist based in Hyderabad, currently practicing as chief consultant physician at The Deccan Hospitals Somajiguda. With over 15 years of clinical experience he is passionate about providing personalized care and working with patients to improve their long-term health and empowering patients through education.
With the mercury rising this season unabated, he is giving us some cool tips to combat the scorching heat.
THE HEAT STROKE STORY : HOW TO KEEP YOUR COOL
As summer temperatures soar, the relentless sun brings more than just a tan – it brings the serious, often fatal risk of sunstroke or heatstroke. With heatwaves becoming more intense due to climate change, understanding this emergency condition is crucial for survival
WHAT IS SUNSTROKE ?
Sunstroke occurs when the body’s cooling system fails, and the core temperature surges, often above 104 f (40 c).This is not just severe dehydration; it’s a medical emergency that can lead to rapid organ failure or death if not treated immediately.
RECOGNIZE THE WARNING SIGNS
Do not confuse sunstroke with simple fatigue. Key symptoms include:
l Neurological changes : confusion, agitation, slurred speech, delirium, coma
l Skin changes: skin feels hot ,dry and red, sometimes losing the ability to sweat despite extreme heat
l Physical distress: throbbing headache, dizziness, nausea, vomitings and rapid pulse
l Others : kidney failure, liver failure, respiratory distress, shock, heart failure, muscle breakdown etc including death.
FIRST AID : ACT FAST
l Move them : take the person to a cool, shaded area or an air conditioned room.
l Cool them : apply cold, wet cloths to the skin, use ice packs on the neck, armpits and groin or immerse them in cool water
l Hydrate them : provide fluids if they are conscious.
l If nothing works rush them to the nearby hospital.
PREVENTION TIPS:
l Stay hydrated : drink plenty of fluids to help your body maintain a normal temperature
l Dress appropriately : wear loose fitting, lightweight clothing, preferably in light colors.
l Avoid peak hours : limit strenuous activity during the hottest parts of the day, generally from 10 am to 4 pm
l Never leave anyone in a car : temperatures in a parked car can rise to fatal levels in just 10 minutes
“PROTECT YOURSELF AND YOUR LOVED ONES. BEING AWARE CAN PREVENT A SUNNY DAY FROM TURNING INTO A TRAGED”

articles.title: Hyderabad Heatwave Fuels Rise In Waterborne Illnesses - urbanacres.in
articles.search_term: heatwave (Adilabad OR "Bhadradri Kothagudem" OR Hyderabad OR Jagtial OR Jangaon OR "Jayashankar Bhoopalpally" OR "Jogulamba Gadwal" OR Kamareddy OR Karimnagar OR Khammam OR "Komaram Bheem Asifabad" OR Mahabubabad OR Mahabubnagar OR Mancherial OR Medak OR Medchal OR Nagarkurnool OR Nalgonda OR Nirmal OR Nizamabad OR Peddapalli OR "Rajanna Sircilla" OR Rangareddy OR Sangareddy OR Siddipet OR Suryapet OR Vikarabad OR Wanaparthy OR "Warangal (Rural)" OR "Warangal (Urban)" OR "Yadadri Bhuvanagiri") Telangana
articles.full_text:
Rising temperatures across Hyderabad are triggering a sharp increase in water-borne diseases and dehydration-related illnesses, placing renewed pressure on the city’s healthcare infrastructure as prolonged heatwave conditions intensify public health risks across urban and peri-urban areas.Hospitals and clinics in Hyderabad and neighbouring districts are reporting a surge in gastroenteritis, diarrhoea, typhoid and kidney-related complications linked to extreme summer heat and unsafe drinking water conditions.
Medical professionals say temperatures crossing 41 degrees Celsius over consecutive days have accelerated bacterial contamination in food and water while worsening dehydration-related illnesses. Healthcare officials noted that densely populated neighbourhoods with irregular water supply and inadequate sanitation infrastructure are among the most vulnerable zones. Public health experts say households relying on stored water, tanker supply and informal cooling beverages face higher risks of contamination during extended heat spells.The Hyderabad heatwave health crisis is increasingly being viewed as an urban resilience challenge rather than only a seasonal medical issue. Climate researchers and urban planners argue that rising temperatures, shrinking green cover and expanding concrete surfaces are amplifying heat-retention effects across the metropolitan region, particularly in high-density residential and commercial corridors. Doctors in government and private hospitals have also reported an increase in dehydration-linked kidney complications during the ongoing heatwave. Medical specialists warn that gastrointestinal infections combined with excessive fluid loss can significantly increase the risk of acute kidney injury, particularly among elderly residents, children and outdoor workers exposed to prolonged heat conditions. Public health authorities have intensified advisories urging residents to consume treated water, avoid stale food and limit outdoor exposure during peak afternoon hours. Officials are also encouraging the use of Oral Rehydration Solutions and improved hygiene practices to reduce infection risks in vulnerable communities.
Urban health researchers say Hyderabad’s recurring summer disease outbreaks reveal deeper infrastructure and planning gaps linked to water access, sanitation and climate adaptation. Several localities continue to face challenges involving sewage seepage into water pipelines, poor drainage systems and inconsistent monitoring of informal food vending networks. In some Secunderabad neighbourhoods, recent gastroenteritis outbreaks were reportedly linked to contamination of drinking water pipelines. Environmental experts further argue that climate-linked health risks are becoming more severe due to rapid urbanisation and declining ecological buffers across Hyderabad. Encroached lakes, reduced tree cover and extensive concretisation have contributed to higher urban temperatures and reduced natural cooling capacity in many neighbourhoods. The state government has activated portions of its Heatwave Action Plan, including cooling centres, public hydration points and emergency medical preparedness measures. However, climate specialists say adaptation strategies alone may not fully address worsening urban heat stress unless cities simultaneously invest in green infrastructure, clean water systems and heat-resilient urban planning.
As Hyderabad faces longer and more intense summer cycles, experts warn that heat-linked illnesses may increasingly become a recurring urban governance challenge affecting productivity, healthcare capacity and public well-being. For a rapidly expanding metropolitan region, the ability to strengthen climate-sensitive public health systems may prove as critical as investments in roads, housing and economic infrastructure.
